      Stock splits
   =20
 =20
The conventional thing to say is that a stock split doesn=E2=80=99t change =
anything important about a company or its stock. You have a share of stock =
that trades at $1,000, you split it 10-for-1, now you have 10 shares that t=
rade at $100 each, nothing has changed, who cares. In the olden days this m=
attered more, because it was cheapest and easiest to buy shares in =E2=80=
=9Cround lots=E2=80=9D of 100 shares; a $100,000 round lot would price a lo=
t of investors out of the stock, while a $10,000 round lot would increase d=
emand for the stock. But in modern markets it is basically as easy to buy o=
ne=C2=A0share as 100, and at most retail brokerages these days it=E2=80=99s=
 just as easy to buy 0.05 shares or whatever other fraction you want. So no=
body is really priced out of a stock by a high-dollar price tag, so splitti=
ng the stock shouldn=E2=80=99t attract new investors, so it shouldn=E2=80=
=99t matter.I have  endorsed that conventional wisdom before, but I think b=
y now I have been more or less talked out of it. Now I want to defend stock=
 splits. Splitting your stock can make the stock go up, and there is a logi=
cal explanation for it. Here=E2=80=99s the news hook,  from Bloomberg News =
today:Recent proposals from Alphabet Inc., Amazon.com Inc. and Tesla Inc. t=
ell us one thing: Stock splits can spark big rallies as retail traders pile=
 in.Tesla surged 8% Monday, adding about $84 billion to the company=E2=80=
=99s market value, after saying it=E2=80=99s planning a second stock split =
in less than two years. Amazon jumped more than 5% the day after announcing=
 a 20-for-1 split this month and the stock has been on a tear ever since.In=
 theory, this shouldn=E2=80=99t happen. A split doesn=E2=80=99t affect a co=
mpany=E2=80=99s business fundamentals, and investors averse to a stock=E2=
=80=99s high price tag can simply buy fractional shares instead. Yet splits=
 are causing day traders to pile in, fueling rallies in these companies=E2=
=80=99 shares.=E2=80=9CWe simply cannot fundamentally explain how a stock s=
plit can add nearly 1.5 times the market cap of General Motors or one full =
Volkswagen=E2=80=99s worth of market cap to Tesla almost instantly,=E2=80=
=9D Morgan Stanley analyst Adam Jonas wrote in a note to clients.=C2=A0But =
let=E2=80=99s try. (Not =E2=80=9Cfundamentally=E2=80=9D but whatever.[1])Wh=
ile the stock market doesn=E2=80=99t really trade in round lots anymore, th=
e=C2=A0options=C2=A0market does: If you want to buy listed call options, yo=
u have to buy them in contracts=C2=A0of 100 shares. (There are weird market=
-structure reasons for this. Most retail stock trades are internalized, wit=
h the retail broker routing your order to some market maker who will fill i=
t from its own inventory, and if it  wants to sell you fractional shares th=
at=E2=80=99s fine. Listed options=C2=A0have to=C2=A0trade on the exchange, =
the exchange has 100-share contracts, and you can=E2=80=99t get around that=
 by buying half a contract from a market maker.) So if you want to buy a Te=
sla call option struck at $1,100 expiring on April 14, you=E2=80=99ll pay a=
bout $5,500 for one contract ($55 per share for 100 shares). If Tesla did a=
 10-for-1 stock split, you could buy options for as little as $550.Again, I=
 used to think that this didn=E2=80=99t matter, because options trading is =
either (1) for professionals, who can afford $5,500 a throw, or (2) for ret=
ail weirdos, who can=E2=80=99t be the driving force of corporate finance. B=
ut I do think that an important lesson of last year=E2=80=99s GameStop Corp=
. meme-stock situation is that retail options weirdos are in fact the drivi=
ng force of corporate finance, or, at least, that=C2=A0retail options tradi=
ng is a key part of being a meme stock.=E2=80=9CIf everyone buys short-date=
d out-of-the-money call options at once, the stock will go up,=E2=80=9D was=
 an important explicit thesis of the Reddit WallStreetBets GameStop enthusi=
asts. So they all bought call options, and the stock went up.=C2=A0The theo=
ry here =E2=80=94=C2=A0the popular term for it is =E2=80=9Cgamma squeeze=E2=
=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0is that when retail traders buy call options, the opt=
ions dealers who sell them the options hedge by buying some of the underlyi=
ng stock. This makes the stock go up. Often, the dealers spend more money o=
n the hedge than the retail traders spend on the option, meaning that buyin=
g an option gives you more bang for your buck =E2=80=94=C2=A0makes the stoc=
k go up more =E2=80=94=C2=A0than just buying the same dollar amount of stoc=
k would have. Also, as the stock goes up, the options dealers have to buy e=
ven=C2=A0more=C2=A0stock to adjust their hedges, in a self-reinforcing cycl=
e.This theory is not right in every particular, dealers=E2=80=99 hedging ca=
n also push the price back=C2=A0down,[2] and there is  some debate about  h=
ow much of this stuff actually occurred, or mattered, in GameStop=E2=80=99s=
 wild week last January. But the point is that an army of small retail inve=
stors=C2=A0YOLOing options to make a stock go up is an important part of me=
me-stock mythology now, and GameStop at least=C2=A0suggests=C2=A0that it ca=
n=C2=A0work.=C2=A0Tesla Inc. is in some ways the original meme stock, and R=
edditors were pushing the gamma-squeeze perpetual-motion theory of Tesla  a=
t least as far back as early 2020. The stock is up about 580% since then.=
=C2=A0And now Tesla=E2=80=99s stock is very expensive, so its options are p=
resumably out of reach for some Redditors; splitting the stock will allow m=
ore retail traders to YOLO more options, which will create more Reddit-y re=
tail enthusiasm, which=C2=A0should be good for the stock.More generally the=
 point that I want to make here is that corporate finance in 2022 is about =
at least four things:=09Doing good business stuff, investing money in busin=
ess projects that have positive expected value, etc.=09Doing good capital-s=
tructure stuff, making sure that you will be able to pay your liabilities w=
hen they come due, etc.=09Appealing to institutional investors with good di=
sclosure,=C2=A0friendly investor-relations professionals, good environmenta=
l, social and governance behavior, etc.=09 Appealing to retail investors wi=
th memes, crypto, Teslas, easily YOLOable options, etc.If you make it easie=
r at the margin for your stock to become a meme stock, then the chance of y=
our stock doubling for no reason goes up from, like, 0.1% to 0.3% or whatev=
er. (The baseline probability for a giant liquid company is pretty low =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0it seems unlikely that Apple Inc. will become a meme stock? =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0but Tesla really is both huge and meme-y so you never know.) Th=
e appeal of your stock to a fundamental institutional investor goes from =
=E2=80=9Cour cash flows are solid, etc.=E2=80=9D to =E2=80=9Cour cash flows=
 are solid, etc., and also you get a lottery ticket on meme-stock enthusias=
m.=E2=80=9D That=C2=A0should=C2=A0make the stock worth more, even now, to e=
veryone.Even more generally. There is a view of the stock market that is li=
ke:=09A share of stock represents fractional ownership of a real business.=
=09In the long run, the value of that share is equal to your share of the f=
ree cash flow of that business.=09The stock price today represents some sor=
t of composite estimate=C2=A0of that future free cash flow.=09At any given =
time, that estimate will probably be wrong for various reasons, but those r=
easons are all bad. They represent ignorance and irrationality and ineffici=
ency.=09In the=C2=A0long run the bad reasons wash out and the stock is wort=
h its fundamental value.But the central argument of  the meme-stock era is:=
=09No, a share of stock represents a token that people can trade for money.=
=09In the long run, it is worth what people will pay for it.=09Things that=
=C2=A0increase the attractiveness of buying it=C2=A0make it more valuable i=
n the long run.=09For various reasons (tradition, the market for corporate =
control, etc.), increasing the cash flows of the underlying company is a ve=
ry important way to increase the attractiveness of buying the stock, but it=
 is not the only one.=09Giving the stock a more appealing ticker symbol, fo=
r instance, adds value.=09Giving away   free popcorn with every stock purch=
ase, why not.=09Having a fun online community of people talking about the s=
tock.=09If the CEO is funny online that definitely helps.=09Sure, make it e=
asier to YOLO options.That is, the =E2=80=9Cnon-fundamental=E2=80=9D things=
 about a stock are not just unpredictable noise; they are real facts about =
the stock market, investors can try to anticipate them, and companies=C2=A0=
can try to control them. You can  build a corporate finance strategy around=
 memes.=C2=A0If splitting your stock makes your stock go up then you should=
 split your stock, not because that will maximize your long-term free cash =
flow but because it will maximize your=C2=A0stock price. Those are differen=
t things!

      MicroStrategy
   =20
 =20
I love MicroStrategy Inc.=E2=80=99s corporate finance strategy, which consi=
sts basically of finding new ways to borrow money to buy Bitcoins. It has d=
one convertible bonds to buy Bitcoins. It did a secured bond last year (sec=
ured by the Bitcoins it bought with that bond=E2=80=99s proceeds, =E2=80=9C=
but excluding MicroStrategy=E2=80=99s existing bitcoins as well as bitcoins=
 and digital assets acquired with the proceeds from existing bitcoins=E2=80=
=9D). And now it is getting a margin loan on some of its Bitcoins =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0presumably not the ones securing that bond =E2=80=94=C2=A0to buy more=
 Bitcoins:Silvergate Bank, a subsidiary of Silvergate Capital Corporation (=
NYSE: SI), the leading provider of innovative financial infrastructure solu=
tions and services for the growing digital currency industry, today announc=
ed it has issued a $205 million term loan under its Silvergate Exchange Net=
work (SEN) Leverage program to MacroStrategy LLC, a subsidiary of MicroStra=
tegy Incorporated (Nasdaq: MSTR) which is the largest independent publicly =
traded analytics and business intelligence company.The interest-only term l=
oan is secured by certain bitcoin held in MacroStrategy=E2=80=99s collatera=
l account with a custodian mutually authorized by Silvergate and MacroStrat=
egy. Under the terms of the agreement, MacroStrategy will use the loan proc=
eeds (i) to purchase bitcoins, (ii) to pay fees, interest, and expenses rel=
ated to the loan transaction, or (iii) for MacroStrategy=E2=80=99s or Micro=
Strategy=E2=80=99s general corporate purposes.Here are MicroStrategy=E2=80=
=99s 8-K=C2=A0and the loan agreement, so now you know what it looks like wh=
en a public company borrows against its Bitcoins. Some highlights of the te=
rms:=09The loan =E2=80=9Cbears interest at a floating rate equal to the Sec=
ured Overnight Financing Rate 30 Day Average as published by the Federal Re=
serve Bank of New York=E2=80=99s website (0.099% as of the Closing Date) pl=
us 3.70%, with a floor of 3.75%.=E2=80=9D (MicroStrategy=E2=80=99s=C2=A07-y=
ear Bitcoin-secured bonds last June were sold=C2=A0at 6.125%.)=09It=E2=80=
=99s a margin loan with daily margining: =E2=80=9CThe Loan was collateraliz=
ed at closing by bitcoin with a value of approximately $820.0 million place=
d in a collateral account with a custodian,=E2=80=9D representing a loan-to=
-value ratio of 25%. If the LTV gets above 25% (that is, if the price of Bi=
tcoin goes down), MicroStrategy has to either pay back some of the loan, pu=
t more Bitcoins into the collateral account, or (as long as the LTV stays b=
elow 35%) pay a 0.25% higher interest rate. If the LTV gets below 25% (that=
 is, if the price of Bitcoin goes up), MicroStrategy can take back some of =
its Bitcoins.=C2=A0This is a fairly small amount of money and a low LTV, bu=
t it does point the way to an exciting future where public-company balance =
sheets can be, like, $10 billion of Bitcoins funded with $8 billion of loan=
s with daily margining. That=E2=80=99s a good way for cryptocurrency volati=
lity to cause real-world volatility.

      Twilio=C2=A0
   =20
 =20
Obviously don=E2=80=99t do this:According to the SEC=E2=80=99s complaint, f=
riends Hari Sure, Lokesh Lagudu and Chotu Pulagam were software engineers a=
t Twilio and had access to various databases relevant to the company=E2=80=
=99s reporting of revenue. As alleged, around March 2020, they learned thro=
ugh the databases that Twilio=E2=80=99s customers had increased their usage=
 of the company=E2=80=99s products and services in response to health measu=
res taken in light of the Covid-19 pandemic, and concluded in a joint chat =
that Twilio=E2=80=99s stock price would =E2=80=9Crise for sure.=E2=80=9DThe=
 SEC=E2=80=99s complaint alleges that despite receiving a company policy th=
at prohibited them from insider trading, Sure, Lagudu and Chotu Pulagam kno=
wingly tipped off, or used the brokerage accounts of, their family and clos=
e friends =E2=80=93 Dileep Kamujula, Sai Nekkalapudi, Abhishek Dharmapurika=
r and Chetan Pulagam =E2=80=93 to trade Twilio options and stock in advance=
 of its May 6, 2020 earnings announcement while in possession of the confid=
ential information concerning customer usage. According to the complaint, t=
he scheme generated more than $1 million in illegal trading profits.That is=
 from a Securities and Exchange Commission enforcement action yesterday aga=
inst three friends who worked at Twilio Inc., as well as various friends an=
d family members of theirs who allegedly traded on their inside knowledge o=
f Twilio=E2=80=99s=C2=A0good pandemic earnings. They allegedly did a number=
 of things that I cannot recommend, including buying short-dated out-of-the=
-money call options on Twilio:In the spring of 2020, Nekkalapudi began to t=
rade Twilio securities using a brokerage account that had been inactive for=
 several years. In the days before the May 6, 2020 earnings announcement, N=
ekkalapudi requested permission to trade options for the first time in her =
brokerage account. Despite never having previously traded Twilio securities=
, Nekkalapudi=E2=80=99s account purchased Twilio call options and shares, e=
ither at the direction of Lagudu or based on tips passed from Lagudu.And br=
agging about insider trading in the group chat:Defendants Sure, Lagudu, and=
 Chotu Pulagam continued to discuss in this chat channel that by March 30, =
2020, Twilio=E2=80=99s quarterly revenue had already crossed the market exp=
ectations for the first quarter (which would end March 31, 2020), and that =
as a result Twilio=E2=80=99s common share price would =E2=80=9Crise for sur=
e=E2=80=9D for two to three days after the quarterly earnings announcement.=
 ...On May 4, 2020 (just two days before the scheduled Twilio earnings anno=
uncement), Sure, Pulagam, and Chotu Pulagam discussed in the chat channel t=
heir anticipation that Twilio=E2=80=99s stock price, which was then trading=
 around $110 per share, would dramatically increase following the earnings =
announcement and readied themselves to sell their own company restricted st=
ock units post-announcement. Sure noted =E2=80=9C[l]ooks like [the stock pr=
ice] is going to be $150,=E2=80=9D to which Chotu Pulagam responded =E2=80=
=9CMiillionaireeeeee.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Don=E2=80=99t do that, the options are =
how they catch you and the chats are how they prove it, etc.Still this is a=
 little bit of a weird case. At one extreme of insider trading, if you work=
 at a company and you find out that it is about to be acquired in a merger,=
 and you buy a lot of short-dated out-of-the-money call options on your com=
pany that will pay off when the merger is announced, that is clearly very b=
ad. If you work in the accounting department and you read a draft earnings =
release that is better than the market expects, and you buy call options, t=
hat is also bad. If you work in the information security department and you=
 find out about a disastrous hack and sell all your stock before the market=
 knows about it, also bad. Big obvious material events where you have privi=
leged information and trade on it, bad.On the other hand if you work in sal=
es at a company, and you are just bopping along early in the quarter, and y=
ou are=C2=A0busy, you=E2=80=99re hearing a lot from customers, the energy i=
s good, you=E2=80=99re closing a lot of sales =E2=80=94=C2=A0can you go buy=
 some of your company=E2=80=99s stock? If things are quiet, can you sell? I=
 don=E2=80=99t know! I think that a very strict interpretation would say th=
at, if you know anything that the general public does not know, you should =
not be trading your company=E2=80=99s stock. But of course you always know =
something=C2=A0that the general public doesn=E2=80=99t know. You come to wo=
rk each day; you can see if your colleagues are happy or sad, busy or bored=
. =E2=80=9CNever buy your company=E2=80=99s stock at all, and only sell und=
er an automatic  10b5-1 plan adopted months in advance=E2=80=9D is a reason=
able way to avoid any risk of prison. But I do think that there is a certai=
n appeal to=C2=A0a rule like =E2=80=9Cmid-level employees of a public compa=
ny should be allowed to buy its stock if they feel confident because work g=
enerally seems to be going well.=E2=80=9D A certain amount of insider tradi=
ng on overall vibes, optimism, things going well, etc., might be good both =
for the efficiency of market prices and for employee morale. And in practic=
e it seems like the SEC tends to go after insider trading on big obvious an=
nouncements, not insider trading on general business optimism. (Not legal a=
dvice!)These guys seem closer to the bad end. They were software engineers =
with access to sales databases, they had a complete view of the company=E2=
=80=99s revenue, the revenue was=C2=A0much=C2=A0better than expectations, t=
hey traded close to (or after) the end of the quarter, they wrote chat mess=
ages like =E2=80=9CMiillionaireeeeee.=E2=80=9D (The chats are the worst par=
t, in that, in the SEC=E2=80=99s summary,=C2=A0they have the tone not of =
=E2=80=9Cwow I love working here, I should buy some stock=E2=80=9D but rath=
er =E2=80=9Cwe are guaranteed to get rich on all this inside information.=
=E2=80=9D) But those databases were not, like,=C2=A0the earnings release; t=
hey certainly weren=E2=80=99t=C2=A0merger news. They were a particularly go=
od statistical summary of =E2=80=9Chey business seems to be going really we=
ll.=E2=80=9D If you have very good data on the business, and the business i=
s going very well, then you can=E2=80=99t trade the stock. I am just not su=
re exactly where that line gets drawn.

      Where are the customers=E2=80=99 yachts?
   =20
 =20
If Russia, or a Russian company or oligarch, owes you money, you might have=
 some trouble getting paid back, what with the harsh sanctions on Russia an=
d the general nervousness that many banks now have about processing payment=
s for Russians. On the other hand if a Russian oligarch owes you money=C2=
=A0secured by a yacht, and you can=C2=A0find the yacht, then, uh,=C2=A0 now=
 you have a yacht?JPMorgan Chase & Co., the biggest U.S. bank, is behind co=
urt moves to seize a superyacht owned by a sanctioned Russian billionaire i=
n Gibraltar.In one of the first public court actions by a bank to enforce o=
n a debt against a sanctioned individual, JPMorgan won a court order author=
izing Gibraltar=E2=80=99s port authority to detain the 73-meter (240-foot) =
yacht Axioma owned by Dmitry Pumpyansky.The 5-deck Axioma, which boasts an =
infinity swimming pool plus a 3D cinema, was impounded last week. Officials=
 in the British territory said that while they wouldn=E2=80=99t normally ha=
ve allowed the yacht to enter Gibraltar waters given Pumpyansky=E2=80=99s o=
wnership, they seized the vessel =E2=80=9Cin the interests of creditors wit=
h proper claims.=E2=80=9DI mean, you sort of have a yacht. In the ordinary =
course what happens when a customer defaults on a yacht loan is that the ba=
nk seizes the yacht, the bank sells the yacht, the bank pays back its loan =
out of the proceeds, and the customer gets whatever is left over. (I mean, =
that is schematically=C2=A0how secured lending works in general; I assume t=
hat yacht loans are similar but a bit more polite.) Here various aspects of=
 that =E2=80=94=C2=A0selling the yacht, giving the excess proceeds to Pumpy=
ansky, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0might be tricky, and the yacht might be in limbo=
 for a bit. I hope JPMorgan will get to, like, host summer-intern parties o=
n the yacht.=C2=A0

   =20
      Money laundering
   =20
 =20
A core difficulty in financial crime=C2=A0is that if you steal $35 million,=
 eventually the authorities are going to notice you having all that money, =
and they are going to ask questions like =E2=80=9Cwhere did you get this $3=
5 million?=E2=80=9D For instance, if you and your boss are investment banke=
rs, and you are allegedly involved in the multibillion-dollar looting of a =
Malaysian state investment fund, and if your boss confesses to everything a=
nd testifies that you stole $35 million of the money for yourself, and the =
prosecutors point to $35 million that suddenly appeared in your wife=E2=80=
=99s mother=E2=80=99s bank account at exactly the moment the Malaysian fund=
 got looted, you will want to have some explanation for that $35 million th=
at is not =E2=80=9Cyes that=E2=80=99s the money I stole.=E2=80=9DFor instan=
ce your explanation might be along the lines of: =E2=80=9CWell, see,=C2=A0m=
y wife befriended my boss=E2=80=99s wife, and my wife invested some money i=
n my boss=E2=80=99s wife=E2=80=99s=C2=A0family=E2=80=99s business, but then=
 when my boss=E2=80=99s wife found out that my boss was cheating on her and=
 that my wife and I both knew about it, she got mad at my wife and forced h=
er to take back her investment with enormous profits, and that=E2=80=99s wh=
at that $26 million is.=E2=80=9D This strikes me as basically a=C2=A0good=
=C2=A0answer? It has flaws ($26 million is not $35 million?), but honestly =
the fact that it is confusing is=C2=A0helpful.=C2=A0It does a nice job of d=
eflecting from the issue. At the start of that sentence, your listeners are=
 thinking about all the money that got stolen; by the end of it they are li=
ke =E2=80=9Cwait who cheated on whom with whom now?=E2=80=9D Anyway maybe i=
t=E2=80=99s true,  I don=E2=80=99t know:The wife of former Goldman Sachs Gr=
oup Inc banker Roger Ng denied that $35 million held in an account in her m=
other=E2=80=99s name was stolen from 1MDB, claiming instead that the money =
was the return on a legitimate investment. ...[Hwee Bin] Lim said on Monday=
 that she befriended [Judy] Chan Leissner after Ng began working under Leis=
sner in 2005. The two couples often socialized, Lim said, and she would als=
o see Chan Leissner at the health club to which both women belonged.Accordi=
ng to Lim, the women=E2=80=99s friendship turned into a chance to invest in=
 Chan Leissner=E2=80=99s family businesses, which included China=E2=80=99s =
largest vineyard. Lim said she and her family initially put $6 million into=
 the venture, which grew considerably.But Lim said the investment came to a=
n end in 2011 after Chan Leissner discovered that Leissner was having an af=
fair with Rohana Rozhan, former chief executive officer of media company As=
tro Malaysia Holdings Bhd, which Lim and Ng had known about. Leissner testi=
fied earlier during the trial that his affair with Rozhan lasted from 2003 =
to 2013.=E2=80=9CJudy called me and she went ballistic over Tim=E2=80=99s r=
elationship with Rohana,=E2=80=9D Lim said. =E2=80=9CShe said, =E2=80=98It=
=E2=80=99s time you should think about divestment.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9DLim tes=
tified that her investment with Chan Leissner had grown to be worth about $=
26 million by then. She said she had hoped it would grow more with an initi=
al public offering Chan Leissner=E2=80=99s family was planning for the vine=
yard, but Chan Leissner insisted on returning the money to Lim and her fami=
ly. ..Prosecutors have noted that was also the same month the first bond of=
fering Goldman handled for 1MDB closed. Lim didn=E2=80=99t explain on the s=
tand Monday how the $26 million became $35 million.=C2=A0Just as a matter o=
f defense-lawyering strategy, the fact that Tim Leissner =E2=80=94=C2=A0Rog=
er Ng=E2=80=99s former boss at Goldman Sachs Group Inc., who has pleaded gu=
ilty to looting 1MDB and is testifying against Ng =E2=80=94=C2=A0is a doubl=
e bigamist presents lots of interesting opportunities to make this trial ab=
out all the adultery rather than all the looting.

 Payments System That Escaped Western Sanctions. Xi Battles Distrust by  Gl=
obal Investors Burned in China Before. Secret World of Pro-Russia Hacking G=

ess Money.If you'd like to get=C2=A0Money=C2=A0Stuff=C2=A0in handy email fo=

] I should add that there are other arguable advantages of a lower dollar s=
tock price. Here are a few:=C2=A0If you pay employees partly in shares, it =
is probably hard to give them fractional shares (harder than it is for Robi=
nhood to do it), so you can more finely tune your employee comp with a lowe=
r share price. If you are a huge company and have a lower dollar price, you=
 can maybe get into=C2=A0the Dow Jones Industrial Average, which is archaic=
ally price-weighted and so excludes very-high-dollar stocks, and maybe bein=
g in the Dow is good for you somehow. There is actually some residual weird=
ness with  odd-lot trading market structure, so it might be better for inst=
itutional traders if your round lots are smaller.=C2=A0There is  some argum=
ent that stock liquidity is maximized at some medium tick size, but the tic=
k size for most U.S. stocks is a penny, and that might be too small to maxi=
mize liquidity in a $1,000 stock; Nasdaq has made the case that a high-doll=
ar stock =E2=80=9Cincreases investor costs, usage of odd-lots, flickering q=
uotations, non-displayed trading that doesn=E2=80=99t support price discove=
ry, and price instability.=E2=80=9D[2] In particular if the option is out-o=
f-the-money and getting close to=C2=A0expiration, its delta will decline, c=
ausing dealers to sell. If every Monday retail traders are buying a ton of =
out-of-the-money Friday-expiry calls, sometimes that will =E2=80=9Cwork=E2=
=80=9D and push the price up until the calls are in the money, but if it do=
esn=E2=80=99t then the dealers=E2=80=99 hedging activity on Thursday is goi=
ng to be mostly selling.

<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Stock splits</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The conventional thing to say is that =
a stock split doesn=E2=80=99t change anything important about a company or =
its stock. You have a share of stock that trades at $1,000, you split it 10=
-for-1, now you have 10 shares that trade at $100 each, nothing has changed=
, who cares. In the olden days this mattered more, because it was cheapest =
and easiest to buy shares in =E2=80=9Cround lots=E2=80=9D of 100 shares; a =
$100,000 round lot would price a lot of investors out of the stock, while a=
 $10,000 round lot would increase demand for the stock. But in modern marke=
ts it is basically as easy to buy one=C2=A0share as 100, and at most retail=
 brokerages these days it=E2=80=99s just as easy to buy 0.05 shares or what=
ever other fraction you want. So nobody is really priced out of a stock by =
a high-dollar price tag, so splitting the stock shouldn=E2=80=99t attract n=
ew investors, so it shouldn=E2=80=99t matter.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">I have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27190509=
.263602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDI=
tMDIvbW9uZXktc3R1ZmYtYmFua2luZy1ib251c2VzLXdlcmUtYmlnLXRoaXMteWVhcj9jbXBpZD=
1CQkQwMzI5MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xld=
HRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMjkmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cBf96b4c89" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;"> endorsed</a> that conventional wisdom before, b=
ut I think by now I have been more or less talked out of it. Now I want to =
defend stock splits. Splitting your stock can make the stock go up, and the=
re is a logical explanation for it. Here=E2=80=99s the news hook, <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27190509.263602/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMjkvdGVzbGEtYW1hem9u=
LXN0b2NrLXNwbGl0cy10cmlnZ2VyLXJldGFpbC1zdGFtcGVkZS10ZWNoLXdhdGNoP2NtcGlkPUJ=
CRDAzMjkyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dG=
VyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMyOSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cB829b878f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;"> from Bloomberg News today</a>:</p><blockquote><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Recent proposals from Alphabet Inc., Amazon.com I=
nc. and Tesla Inc. tell us one thing: Stock splits can spark big rallies as=
 retail traders pile in.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tesla surged 8% Mo=
nday, adding about $84 billion to the company=E2=80=99s market value, after=
 saying it=E2=80=99s planning a second stock split in less than two years. =
Amazon jumped more than 5% the day after announcing a 20-for-1 split this m=
onth and the stock has been on a tear ever since.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">In theory, this shouldn=E2=80=99t happen. A split doesn=E2=80=99t af=
fect a company=E2=80=99s business fundamentals, and investors averse to a s=
tock=E2=80=99s high price tag can simply buy fractional shares instead. Yet=
 splits are causing day traders to pile in, fueling rallies in these compan=
ies=E2=80=99 shares.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe simply can=
not fundamentally explain how a stock split can add nearly 1.5 times the ma=
rket cap of General Motors or one full Volkswagen=E2=80=99s worth of market=
 cap to Tesla almost instantly,=E2=80=9D Morgan Stanley analyst Adam Jonas =
wrote in a note to clients.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">But let=E2=80=99s try. (Not =E2=80=9Cfundamentally=E2=80=9D but whateve=
r.[1])</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While the stock market doesn=E2=80=
=99t really trade in round lots anymore, the=C2=A0<em>options=C2=A0</em>mar=
ket does: If you want to buy listed call options, you have to buy them in c=
ontracts=C2=A0of 100 shares. (There are <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/27190509.263602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubmJlci5vcmcvcGFwZXJzL=
3cyOTg4Mz91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bnR3aCZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bnR3=
ZzE5/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB28bab826" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">weird market-structure reas=
ons</a> for this. Most retail stock trades are internalized, with the retai=
l broker routing your order to some market maker who will fill it from its =
own inventory, and if it <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com=
/click/27190509.263602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGlj=
bGVzLzIwMTktMTAtMTgvaGFsZi1hLXNoYXJlLW9mLXN0b2NrLWlzLWJldHRlci10aGFuLW5vdGh=
pbmc_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMyOTIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cm=
NlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwMzI5JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cBcadd1e97" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> wants to sell you fractional shares=
</a> that=E2=80=99s fine. Listed options=C2=A0<em>have to=C2=A0</em>trade o=
n the exchange, the exchange has 100-share contracts, and you can=E2=80=99t=
 get around that by buying half a contract from a market maker.) So if you =
want to buy a Tesla call option struck at $1,100 expiring on April 14, you=
=E2=80=99ll pay about $5,500 for one contract ($55 per share for 100 shares=
). If Tesla did a 10-for-1 stock split, you could buy options for as little=
 as $550.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Again, I used to think that this =
didn=E2=80=99t matter, because options trading is either (1) for profession=
als, who can afford $5,500 a throw, or (2) for retail weirdos, who can=E2=
=80=99t be the driving force of corporate finance. But I do think that an i=
mportant lesson of last year=E2=80=99s GameStop Corp. meme-stock situation =
is that retail options weirdos are in fact the driving force of corporate f=
inance, or, at least, that=C2=A0retail options trading is a key part of bei=
ng a meme stock.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CIf everyone buys =
short-dated out-of-the-money call options at once, the stock will go up,=E2=
=80=9D was an important explicit thesis of the Reddit WallStreetBets GameSt=
op enthusiasts. So they all bought call options, and the stock went up.=C2=
=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27190509.263602=
/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDEtMjUvdG=
hlLWdhbWUtbmV2ZXItc3RvcHM_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMyOTIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1b=
T1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwMzI5JnV0bV9jYW1wYWln=
bj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe9c7c1c9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" =
itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">The theory here=
</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0the popular term for it is =E2=80=9Cgamma squeeze=E2=80=
=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0is that when retail traders buy call options, the option=
s dealers who sell them the options hedge by buying some of the underlying =
stock. This makes the stock go up. Often, the dealers spend more money on t=
he hedge than the retail traders spend on the option, meaning that buying a=
n option gives you more bang for your buck =E2=80=94=C2=A0makes the stock g=
o up more =E2=80=94=C2=A0than just buying the same dollar amount of stock w=
ould have. Also, as the stock goes up, the options dealers have to buy even=
=C2=A0<em>more=C2=A0</em>stock to adjust their hedges, in a self-reinforcin=
g cycle.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This theory is not right in every =
particular, dealers=E2=80=99 hedging can also push the price back=C2=A0<em>=
down</em>,[2] and there is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/27190509.263602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydG=
ljbGVzLzIwMjEtMTAtMTkvbWF0dC1sZXZpbmUtcy1tb25leS1zdHVmZi1nYW1lc3RvcC1iYWZmb=
GVzLXRoZS1zZWMtdG9vP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMjkyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1h=
aWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMyOSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9=
uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf942e158" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" items=
cope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> some debate</a> abo=
ut <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27190509.263602=
/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDItMTYvcG=
VvcGxlLWFyZS13b3JyaWVkLWFib3V0LWJsb2NrLXRyYWRlcz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzI5MjJfTU9OR=
VlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0y=
MjAzMjkmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBcde6182f" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;"> how much</a> of this stuff actually occurred, or mattered, in Game=
Stop=E2=80=99s wild week last January. But the point is that an army of sma=
ll retail investors=C2=A0YOLOing options to make a stock go up is an import=
ant part of meme-stock mythology now, and GameStop at least=C2=A0<em>sugges=
ts=C2=A0</em>that it can=C2=A0work.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">T=
esla Inc. is in some ways the original meme stock, and Redditors were pushi=
ng the gamma-squeeze perpetual-motion theory of Tesla <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27190509.263602/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21=
iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjAtMDItMjYvcmVkZGl0LXBvc3RlcnMtcGljay=
10aGUtc3RvY2tzLW5vdz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzI5MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtY=
WlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMjkmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1v=
bmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBcb1b4f7c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" items=
cope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> at least as far bac=
k as early 2020</a>. The stock is up about 580% since then.=C2=A0And now Te=
sla=E2=80=99s stock is very expensive, so its options are presumably out of=
 reach for some Redditors; splitting the stock will allow more retail trade=
rs to YOLO more options, which will create more Reddit-y retail enthusiasm,=
 which=C2=A0should be good for the stock.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">M=
ore generally the point that I want to make here is that corporate finance =
in 2022 is about at least four things:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: dec=
imal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5=
px;">Doing good business stuff, investing money in business projects that h=
ave positive expected value, etc.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=
Doing good capital-structure stuff, making sure that you will be able to pa=
y your liabilities when they come due, etc.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bott=
om: 5px;">Appealing to institutional investors with good disclosure,=C2=A0f=
riendly investor-relations professionals, good environmental, social and go=
vernance behavior, etc.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"><a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27190509.263602/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMTEtMDQvbWFjeS1zLXRhcmd=
ldGVkLWJ5LW1lbWUtYWN0aXZpc3RzP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMjkyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZW=
RpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMyOSZ1dG1fY2Ftc=
GFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7fde1586" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Appealing=
 to retail investors</a> with memes, crypto, Teslas, easily YOLOable option=
s, etc.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you make it easier at the =
margin for your stock to become a meme stock, then the chance of your stock=
 doubling for no reason goes up from, like, 0.1% to 0.3% or whatever. (The =
baseline probability for a giant liquid company is pretty low =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0it seems unlikely that Apple Inc. will become a meme stock? =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but Tesla really is both huge and meme-y so you never know.) The appeal =
of your stock to a fundamental institutional investor goes from =E2=80=9Cou=
r cash flows are solid, etc.=E2=80=9D to =E2=80=9Cour cash flows are solid,=
 etc., and also you get a lottery ticket on meme-stock enthusiasm.=E2=80=9D=
 That=C2=A0<em>should=C2=A0</em>make the stock worth more, even now, to eve=
